disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Known Type 1 diabetes with partially skipped insulin doses during an intercurrent illness
trigger_question_examples: Do you have diabetes, and have you been able to take your insulin as usual?
Have you missed any insulin doses recently?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: I have Type 1 diabetes and I skipped some of my insulin yesterday because I couldn't keep food down.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Extreme thirst and polyuria over the past 24 hours
trigger_question_examples: Have you been drinking a lot more than usual, or urinating much more?
Does your mouth feel unusually dry or thirsty despite drinking a lot?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Yes, I've been drinking huge amounts of water and going to the bathroom constantly.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Parent reports increasing drowsiness and fast, deep breathing
trigger_question_examples: Has anyone noticed her breathing looking unusual, like fast or deep?
Is she becoming more drowsy or hard to wake?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: [Parent reports] She's breathing really fast and deep, and she seems more out of it than before.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Repeated vomiting with diffuse abdominal pain over the last 8 hours
trigger_question_examples: How many times have you vomited, and where is the stomach pain?
Is the abdominal pain constant or crampy, and all over or in one spot?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: I've thrown up several times and my whole stomach aches.

patient_initiated_exchanges.patient_text: [Parent] Should I just give her a big catch-up dose of insulin now to bring her sugar down?
patient_initiated_exchanges.expected_ai_doctor_response_quality: Should advise against giving a large uncontrolled insulin dose at home, explaining that this presentation (missed insulin, polyuria/polydipsia, vomiting, deep rapid breathing, lethargy) is highly concerning for diabetic ketoacidosis, which requires monitored intravenous fluids and a carefully titrated insulin infusion in hospital alongside electrolyte (particularly potassium) monitoring, and should recommend immediate 000 ambulance dispatch instead.
